sources.citation: Olson, D. H. (2000). Circumplex model of marital and family systems. Journal of Family Therapy, 22(2), 144–167.
sources.relevance: Foundational model defining family cohesion as emotional bonding among family members, directly informing the measurement of perceived unity and relationship quality within families.
sources.key_finding: Family cohesion, along with flexibility and communication, is a core dimension predicting family functioning, with balanced levels associated with healthier outcomes.

sources.citation: Villarreal, R., Blozis, S. A., & Widaman, K. F. (2005). Factorial invariance of a pan-Hispanic familism scale. Hispanic Journal of Behavioral Sciences, 27(4), 409–425.
sources.relevance: Examines familism — the strong identification and attachment to nuclear and extended family — among Hispanic populations, directly relevant to measuring family bond quality in Mexico.
sources.key_finding: A multidimensional familism scale demonstrated factorial invariance across Hispanic subgroups, confirming that family cohesion and obligation are culturally salient and reliably measurable constructs.

sources.citation: Esteinou, R. (2008). La familia nuclear en México: Lecturas de su modernidad, siglos XVI al XX. CIESAS.
sources.relevance: Provides historical and sociological analysis of the Mexican nuclear family, including the values and relational dynamics that sustain family unity in the Mexican context.
sources.key_finding: Mexican family cohesion has been shaped by a blend of Catholic moral tradition and modernization processes, with emotional bonds and shared values remaining central to family unity.

sources.citation: Rivera-Heredia, M. E., & Andrade-Palos, P. (2010). Escala de evaluación de las relaciones intrafamiliares (E.R.I.). Uaricha, Revista de Psicología, 14, 12–29.
sources.relevance: Develops and validates a Mexican-specific scale for assessing intrafamily relationships, including dimensions of union/support, expression, and difficulties.
sources.key_finding: The E.R.I. scale demonstrated strong psychometric properties in Mexican samples, confirming that family union-support, emotional expression, and conflict are distinct measurable dimensions of family relationship quality.

sources.citation: Ariza, M., & de Oliveira, O. (2001). Familias en transición y marcos conceptuales en redefinición. Papeles de Población, 7(28), 9–39.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the transformation of family structures in Latin America and the conceptual frameworks needed to understand normative ideals versus empirical family diversity in Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Mexican families are undergoing significant structural diversification, yet normative ideals of the nuclear, two-parent family persist as dominant aspirational models, creating tension between ideology and lived experience.

sources.citation: Quilodrán, J. (2003). La familia, referentes en transición. Papeles de Población, 9(37), 51–82.
sources.relevance: Examines how normative references about family have shifted in Mexico, including changing attitudes toward cohabitation, single parenthood, and non-traditional family forms.
sources.key_finding: Despite demographic transitions leading to greater family diversity, Mexican public opinion retains strong normative preferences for traditional family arrangements, though younger cohorts show increasing pluralism.

sources.citation: Robichaux, D. (2007). Familia y diversidad en América Latina: Estudios de casos. CLACSO.
sources.relevance: Provides comparative case studies of family diversity across Latin America, including Mexico, challenging the assumption of a single normative family model.
sources.key_finding: Latin American family systems exhibit significant regional and ethnic diversity that is often obscured by dominant normative discourses favoring the Western nuclear family ideal.

sources.citation: Esteinou, R. (2004). El surgimiento de la familia nuclear en México. Estudios de Historia Novohispana, 31, 99–136.
sources.relevance: Traces the historical emergence of the nuclear family ideal in Mexico, providing context for understanding contemporary normative beliefs about ideal family structure.
sources.key_finding: The nuclear family ideal in Mexico was consolidated through colonial-era Catholic doctrine and state modernization policies, becoming deeply embedded in cultural normativity.

causal_relationships.related_construct: attitudes toward human rights (e.g., same-sex families)

sources.citation: Sabogal, F., Marín, G., Otero-Sabogal, R., Marín, B. V., & Perez-Stable, E. J. (1987). Hispanic familism and acculturation: What changes and what doesn't? Hispanic Journal of Behavioral Sciences, 9(4), 397–412.
sources.relevance: Identifies familial obligations as a core dimension of Hispanic familism, including perceived duties of children toward aging parents and reciprocal expectations across generations.
sources.key_finding: Familial obligations — the belief that family members have a duty to provide material and emotional support — remain robust across acculturation levels among Hispanic populations.

sources.citation: Montes de Oca, V. (2004). Envejecimiento y protección familiar en México: Límites y potencialidades del apoyo al interior del hogar. In M. Ariza & O. de Oliveira (Eds.), Imágenes de la familia en el cambio de siglo. UNAM.
sources.relevance: Directly examines intergenerational support obligations in Mexico, focusing on elder care expectations and the limits of family-based social protection.
sources.key_finding: Mexican families remain the primary source of elder care, but urbanization, migration, and women's labor force participation are straining traditional intergenerational support systems.

sources.citation: Robles-Silva, L. (2007). La invisibilidad del cuidado a los enfermos crónicos: Un estudio cualitativo en el barrio de Oblatos. Editorial Universitaria.
sources.relevance: Qualitative study of caregiving obligations within Mexican families, revealing gendered and generational dynamics in who provides care and why.
sources.key_finding: Caregiving for chronically ill family members in Mexico falls disproportionately on women and is sustained by deeply held norms of filial obligation, often at significant personal cost.

sources.citation: Wong, R., & Higgins, M. (2007). Dynamics of intergenerational assistance in middle- and old-age in Mexico. In J. L. Angel & K. E. Whitfield (Eds.), The health of aging Hispanics (pp. 99–120). Springer.
sources.relevance: Quantitatively analyzes patterns of intergenerational transfers (financial, instrumental, emotional) in Mexico using longitudinal data.
sources.key_finding: Intergenerational assistance in Mexico flows bidirectionally but is shaped by household structure, proximity, and economic need, with older adults both giving and receiving support.

causal_relationships.related_construct: aging population and elder care needs

sources.citation: Ojeda, N. (2007). Matrimonio y unión libre en la percepción de adolescentes mexicanos radicados en Tijuana. Frontera Norte, 19(37), 41–68.
sources.relevance: Examines Mexican attitudes toward marriage versus cohabitation, including motivations for partnering and normative expectations around conjugal unions.
sources.key_finding: Mexican adolescents increasingly view cohabitation as a legitimate alternative to marriage, though marriage retains strong normative value, particularly among women and more religious respondents.

sources.citation: Amato, P. R. (2010). Research on divorce: Continuing trends and new developments. Journal of Marriage and Family, 72(3), 650–666.
sources.relevance: Comprehensive review of divorce research including attitudes toward divorce, causes of marital dissolution, and tolerance of infidelity — all directly measured in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Attitudes toward divorce have become more permissive over time, but economic stress, infidelity, and communication problems remain the most commonly cited causes of marital dissolution across cultures.

sources.citation: García, B., & Rojas, O. (2002). Los hogares latinoamericanos durante la segunda mitad del siglo XX: Una perspectiva sociodemográfica. Estudios Demográficos y Urbanos, 17(2), 261–288.
sources.relevance: Analyzes changes in household and conjugal union formation patterns in Latin America, including Mexico, providing demographic context for attitudinal shifts.
sources.key_finding: Latin American conjugal unions have diversified significantly, with rising cohabitation, delayed marriage, and increasing divorce rates reflecting broader socioeconomic and cultural transformations.

sources.citation: Hirsch, J. S. (2003). A courtship after marriage: Sexuality and love in Mexican transnational families. University of California Press.
sources.relevance: Ethnographic study of conjugal relationships in Mexican families, including attitudes toward fidelity, partnership expectations, and the impact of migration on marital dynamics.
sources.key_finding: Mexican conjugal relationships are shifting from models based on respect and obligation toward companionate ideals emphasizing emotional intimacy and mutual trust, though infidelity norms remain gendered.

causal_relationships.related_construct: human rights attitudes (e.g., divorce rights, domestic violence)

sources.citation: Schönpflug, U. (2001). Intergenerational transmission of values: The role of transmission belts. Journal of Cross-Cultural Psychology, 32(2), 174–185.
sources.relevance: Theoretical framework for understanding how values are transmitted from parents to children, directly relevant to the construct's focus on the family as a moral socialization institution.
sources.key_finding: Value transmission is most effective when parents are intentional, when the cultural context supports the values, and when parent-child relationship quality is high.

sources.citation: Rojas, O. L. (2016). Mujeres, hombres y vida familiar en México: Género y desigualdad. El Colegio de México.
sources.relevance: Examines the role of Mexican families in transmitting gendered values and moral orientations, including the influence of religion and institutional context.
sources.key_finding: Mexican families serve as primary agents of value socialization, but the values transmitted are increasingly contested as women's roles change and institutional influences (church, media, school) compete with family influence.

sources.citation: Schwartz, S. H. (2012). An overview of the Schwartz theory of basic values. Online Readings in Psychology and Culture, 2(1), 1–20.
sources.relevance: Provides the dominant theoretical framework for classifying human values (e.g., tradition, benevolence, conformity) that underlie family value transmission processes.
sources.key_finding: Values are organized in a universal circular structure where adjacent values are compatible and opposing values conflict, and family socialization is a key mechanism for value acquisition.

sources.citation: Alduncin, E. (2004). Los valores de los mexicanos. Banamex.
sources.relevance: Large-scale empirical study of Mexican values including family-related values, providing direct context for understanding what values Mexicans prioritize and transmit within families.
sources.key_finding: Family remains the most valued institution among Mexicans, with respect, honesty, and responsibility consistently ranked as the most important values to transmit to children.

sources.citation: Díaz-Guerrero, R. (2003). Bajo las garras de la cultura: Psicología del mexicano 2. Trillas.
sources.relevance: Classic work on Mexican cultural psychology examining how family-based sociocultural premises (e.g., obedience, respect, affiliation) shape personality and value orientations.
sources.key_finding: Mexican culture transmits a distinctive set of sociocultural premises through the family, including strong affiliative values and respect for authority, which serve as foundational scripts for social behavior.
